meta.title: B.A.S.I.C. S.T.A.R.T. Framework
meta.source: GenAI Prompts to Help You Build OT/ICS Cybersecurity
meta.author: Mike Holcomb / UtilSec, LLC
meta.verificationNote: Framework pillar names, structure, and prompt architecture reproduced from published material by Mike Holcomb (UtilSec, LLC). Plan section content and KPI examples are derived from the source document. All outputs must be validated by certified OT professionals against current standards before operational use.

framework.fullExpansion: Backup & Recovery, Asset Inventory, Secure Network Architecture, Incident Response Planning, Continuous Vulnerability Management — Secure Remote Access, Training & Awareness, Risk Assessments, Real-Time Network Monitoring, Teamwork (IT/OT Partnership)
framework.description: A practitioner-focused 10-pillar framework for OT/ICS Cybersecurity Managers to build or improve a foundational cybersecurity program. Each pillar delivers a structured AI prompt that drives the generation of customised, sector-specific plans complete with measurable KPI frameworks.

promptArchitecture.phase1: Discovery & Context Gathering — AI asks environment-specific questions and waits for answers before generating any plan. Typically 4–6 questions covering sector type, current maturity, constraints, and reporting audience.
promptArchitecture.phase2: Strategy Generation — Comprehensive plan tailored to Phase 1 responses. Structured into plan sections with sub-topics addressing IT/OT delineation, system-specific playbooks, metrics, and automation.
promptArchitecture.phase3: Next Steps — 1–2 targeted follow-up questions to refine the theoretical architecture into an actionable project roadmap.

pillars.name: Backup & Recovery
pillars.description: Design a resilient OT Backup and Recovery program structured around the 3-2-1-1-0 framework adapted for industrial environments. Addresses PLC logic, HMI configurations, SCADA servers, and historian data with recovery sequencing that preserves process safety.
pillars.keyPrinciple: Recovery order must follow the Purdue Model: network infrastructure first, then SCADA/DCS, then PLCs/RTUs. A PLC restored to the wrong state into a running process is more dangerous than the original attack.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
SCADA, DCS, PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, Engineering Workstations
Minutes, hours, or days
Fully isolated, converged IT/OT networks, shared Active Directory
Ransomware, insider threat, hardware failure
Plant Managers, Board of Directors, NACSA Auditors

planSections.title: The OT/ICS 3-2-1-1-0 Architecture
keyTopics: 3 copies of data: primary on HMI/EWS, backup on IDMZ server, archive on offline media
2 different media types: OT-rated NAS for operational backup, encrypted USB/tape for cold archive
1 off-site copy: secure transfer out of plant via data diode or IDMZ-hosted SFTP, respecting Purdue Model
1 immutable / offline copy: air-gapped or write-once media that ransomware cannot reach across the network
0 errors (verification): automated hash verification + scheduled restoration testing in isolated testbed — never in production

planSections.title: IT vs. OT Tie-ins & Delineation
keyTopics: Where shared IT infrastructure (backup servers, tape libraries) can be leveraged vs. where OT must remain isolated
Network pathways through DMZ or unidirectional gateways for backup data traversal
IAM controls to prevent compromised IT credentials from deleting or encrypting OT backup copies

planSections.title: System-Specific Backup Playbooks
keyTopics: Level 0/1 — PLCs/RTUs: logic programs, setpoints, firmware images, parameter files
Level 2/3 — HMIs, SCADA servers, Engineering Workstations: full OS + application backup
Level 3 — Data Historians: database snapshots, tag configuration, trend archives

planSections.title: Incident Response & Restoration Workflow
keyTopics: Recovery sequencing: network gear → SCADA/DCS servers → PLCs/RTUs → historian
Decision tree: when to restore vs. rebuild from known-good media
Manual operation procedures to maintain process safety during OT outage

planSections.title: Effective Metrics & KPIs
keyTopics: Leading: % of Level 1 PLCs with verified logic backups in last 30 days
Leading: % of backups with successful automated hash verification this month
Lagging: Mean Time to Recover (MTTR) for critical HMIs during testbed simulations
Lagging: Number of failed restoration tests requiring plan update
Audience alignment: Uptime Readiness for Plant Managers vs. Ransomware Resilience for the Board

leading: % of Level 1 PLCs with verified logic backups in the last 30 days
% of SCADA server backups with successful automated hash verification
Frequency of successful immutable vault write-test verifications
lagging: Mean Time to Recover (MTTR) for critical HMIs during testbed simulations
Number of backup failures discovered during quarterly restoration drills
Time elapsed since last successful full-environment restoration test

pillars.name: Asset Management
pillars.description: Design a Metrics and KPI tracking program to measure the health and maturity of OT asset inventory. Produces audience-specific dashboards for Board, Plant Management, and OT SecOps, focused on Purdue Level 1–3 assets with compensating controls rather than IT-centric patch counts.
pillars.keyPrinciple: You cannot protect what you cannot see. Asset inventory in OT must be passive — active scanning can crash fragile PLCs. Discovery must precede every other security initiative.

discoveryQuestions.example: Board of Directors, Plant/Site Managers, OT Security Operations team, Compliance Auditors
No metrics, highly manual spreadsheet tracking, or automated dashboards via SIEM/OT monitoring tools
IEC 62443, NERC CIP, NACSA Act 854
Emphasis on Backup & Recovery and Secure Network Architecture programs already in place

planSections.title: Audience-Specific Dashboards
keyTopics: Executive/Board: risk burn-down rate, compliance posture, financial exposure from unmanaged devices
Plant/Site Manager: days since last passive scan update, percentage of unmanaged devices per site, backup coverage per asset
Tactical/SecOps: anomalous OT protocol traffic per asset, firewall rule violations per zone, Mean Time to Detect/Respond in OT

planSections.title: Leading vs. Lagging OT Indicators
keyTopics: Asset Management: % of Purdue Level 1–3 assets actively monitored vs. estimated total
Backup & Recovery: % of Crown Jewel PLCs/DCS with verified, immutable backups in last 30 days
Vulnerability Management: % of critical vulnerabilities mitigated by network segmentation or compensating controls (not direct patching)
Identity: % of OT remote access sessions utilising MFA and jump servers

planSections.title: Data Collection & Automation Strategy
keyTopics: Passive monitoring tools (Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi) as the primary discovery source — no active scanning
Ingestion of OT-specific firewall logs and IDMZ traffic to a central reporting tool
Baseline establishment for month-over-month trend reporting

planSections.title: The 'So What?' Factor — Driving Action
keyTopics: Linking red metrics directly to budget requests and remediation projects
Pre-defined playbooks for when a metric turns red (e.g., unmanaged device detected → quarantine VLAN + investigation ticket)

leading: % of Purdue Level 1–3 assets in the active passive-monitoring inventory vs. estimated total
% of new OT assets appearing in network traffic that are in the authorised device register
lagging: Number of unmanaged/unknown devices discovered on OT network this month
Age of oldest unreviewed asset entry in the inventory register

pillars.name: Secure Network Architecture
pillars.description: Design a defensible Secure OT Network Architecture implementing the IT/OT Industrial DMZ (IDMZ), IEC 62443 Zones and Conduits methodology for internal segmentation, and a managed ACL lifecycle — with metrics to prove its efficacy.
pillars.keyPrinciple: The Industrial DMZ is not optional — it is the single most impactful architectural control for OT. No traffic should route directly between the enterprise (Levels 4/5) and the industrial network (Levels 0–3) without transiting through IDMZ-hosted services.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Completely flat, basic IT/OT VLAN separation, or fully deployed Purdue Model with IDMZ
Cisco, Palo Alto, Fortinet, Check Point, Nozomi, Claroty
Do vendors or engineers require remote access to the plant floor? How is it handled today?
Critical safety systems or time-sensitive protocols like GOOSE or PROFINET
Plant Managers, Board, or NACSA Auditors

planSections.title: The IT/OT Boundary & Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)
keyTopics: Two-firewall IDMZ architecture: OT-side NGFW + IT-side NGFW with IDMZ hosted services between them
No direct routing: all IT-OT data exchange flows through IDMZ proxy/relay services
Data diodes vs. NGFWs: unidirectional gateways for historian replication; NGFW for control-plane traffic
Secure remote access termination in IDMZ — user laptops never touch Level 0–3 directly

planSections.title: Internal Segmentation — Zones and Conduits (IEC 62443-3-2)
keyTopics: Micro-segmentation: separate operational processes into distinct zones (e.g., packaging zone isolated from mixing zone)
Safety system isolation: SIS physically and logically separated from Basic Process Control System (BPCS)
Wireless & IIoT: 5G, Wi-Fi, and IIoT sensors on isolated wireless DMZ that does not bridge segmented zones

planSections.title: ACL Management & Firewall Rule Lifecycle
keyTopics: Default deny posture in legacy environments that rely on broadcast traffic
90–180 day rule review cadence with documented business justification for every permit rule
Expiration dates on temporary rules; automatic escalation for rules not reviewed on schedule

planSections.title: Additional Architectural Resiliency
keyTopics: Out-of-Band (OOB) management network for switches and firewalls — usable during an active cyber event
High Availability (HA) firewall pairs: failure must not result in loss of view or loss of control for plant operators

planSections.title: Effective Metrics & KPIs
keyTopics: % of firewall rules with 'Any/Any' configurations — target: 0%
Number of unauthorised cross-zone traffic attempts blocked per week
% of remote access sessions transiting through IDMZ jump host vs. direct VPNs

leading: % of firewall rules with open Any/Any source or destination — target: 0%
% of remote access sessions transiting IDMZ jump host vs. direct plant VPN
lagging: Number of unauthorised cross-zone traffic attempts blocked per week
Number of firewall rules past their scheduled review date

pillars.name: Incident Response Planning
pillars.description: Build a proactive, highly operationalised OT Incident Response program grounded in the SANS Top 5 Critical Controls for ICS. Includes sector-specific historical threat intelligence, IT/OT severance protocols, cyber-physical Tabletop Exercises (TTX), and measurable IR KPIs.
pillars.keyPrinciple: In OT, physical safety and process availability outrank data confidentiality. The triage question is not 'what data was stolen?' but 'is the process still safe?' — IR plans that do not account for this will fail at the worst possible moment.

discoveryQuestions.example: Critical for the historical threat intelligence phase
No formal plan, an IT plan being reused for OT, or a dedicated but untested OT IR plan
Do plant managers and safety engineers participate in cyber planning, or is it IT-driven?
Can you physically or logically sever the IT/OT connection without halting production?
Plant Managers, Board, or Regulators (e.g., NACSA s26 notification)

planSections.title: Sector-Specific Threat Intelligence — Historical Analysis
keyTopics: Top 3–4 historical OT incidents targeting your sector with kill chain analysis
For each incident: the attack vector, how SANS Top 5 controls would have broken the kill chain
Adversary TTPs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS techniques

planSections.title: The OT IR Plan — SANS Top 5 Integration
keyTopics: ICS-specific triage: physical safety first, process availability second, data confidentiality third
IT/OT Severance protocol: when and how to physically disconnect IT and OT networks, and who has authority
ICS network visibility: passive PCAP/OT SIEM investigation procedures without active scanning of PLCs
Secure remote access lockdown playbook: terminate all vendor VPNs and jump sessions the moment an anomaly is detected
Post-incident root-cause analysis driving compensating controls and vulnerability remediation

planSections.title: Cyber-Physical Tabletop Exercise (TTX) Programme
keyTopics: Scenario design based on historical incidents from Section 1 (sector-specific realism)
Engineering inject: introduce physical plant injects mid-exercise to force IT/OT collaboration (e.g., 'The HMI says valve is closed, but pressure gauge shows it is building — who do you trust?')
Quarterly mini-tabletops; annual full-scale exercise with Action After Review (AAR)

planSections.title: Effective Metrics & KPIs
keyTopics: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) in the OT environment
IT/OT Severance execution time during drills (target: < 15 minutes)
% of plant operators participating in annual TTX
NACSA s26 notification readiness: time from detection to regulatory notification during drill

leading: % of plant operators and shift supervisors who have participated in an OT-specific IR TTX in the last 12 months
IT/OT severance execution time measured during quarterly drill (minutes)
lagging: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) an anomaly in the OT environment
Time from initial detection to NACSA s26 regulatory notification (target: < 6 hours)
Number of TTX Action Items closed within 90 days of exercise

pillars.name: Continuous Vulnerability Management
pillars.description: Design a foundational, safety-first Vulnerability Management program for OT. Addresses the core challenge that 'patch everything' is not an option — prioritising compensating controls, logical isolation, and risk-based scoring over CVSS-driven IT patching cycles.
pillars.keyPrinciple: CVSS scores fail in OT because they do not account for process criticality (a CVSS-5.0 vulnerability on the chlorination dosing PLC is more dangerous than a CVSS-9.8 on an offline engineering workstation). Scoring must include Process Criticality, Connectivity, and Exploitability in OT context.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Detailed automated list with firmware versions, or starting from scratch
Annual shutdowns only, or frozen legacy systems that can never be updated
Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi (passive), or manual vendor disclosures only
Engineering Operations, Board, Compliance Auditors

planSections.title: OT Vulnerability Discovery Workflow
keyTopics: Passive observation: traffic analysis + firmware version matching against ICS-CERT / CISA advisories — never active Nessus-style scans
Vendor-driven intelligence: Rockwell, Siemens, Schneider Electric security portals for product-specific advisories
'Insecure by Design' identification: assets with clear-text protocols (Modbus, DNP3 without authentication) or default credentials

planSections.title: Risk-Based Prioritisation — Moving Beyond CVSS
keyTopics: OT Risk Score = (Process Criticality × 3) + (Connectivity Score × 2) + (OT Exploitability × 1)
Process Criticality: does this asset directly control safety, production, or environmental outcomes?
Connectivity: is it air-gapped, behind an IDMZ, or exposed to the IT network with dual NICs?
OT Exploitability: is the vulnerability exploitable via Modbus, DNP3, or other protocols in use on the plant?

planSections.title: Mitigation Hierarchy & Compensating Controls
keyTopics: Virtual patching: IPS/IDS signatures at network boundary to block exploit attempts without touching the device
Logical isolation: move vulnerable assets into restricted micro-segmented VLANs
Configuration hardening: disable unnecessary services, close unused ports, enforce strict ACLs
Manual patching: firmware updates during planned maintenance windows with full backup and rollback tested first

planSections.title: Management of Change (MOC) & Vulnerability Ledger
keyTopics: Vulnerability Ledger: formally document every known-unpatched vulnerability with business justification and compensating controls
MOC integration: any change to an OT asset must trigger a vulnerability re-assessment of the impacted zone
Testing protocol: validate patches in isolated testbed environment before deploying to production — always

leading: % of critical vulnerabilities with documented, active compensating controls in the Vulnerability Ledger
% of ICS-CERT advisories reviewed and triaged within 5 business days of publication
lagging: Average age of high-risk vulnerabilities on unsegmented OT assets (target: reduce month over month)
Mean Time to Mitigate (via compensating control) vs. Mean Time to Patch (for patchable assets)

pillars.name: Secure Remote Access
pillars.description: Design a highly secure, zero-trust-aligned OT Remote Access programme that enforces the 'Protocol Break' — ensuring no remote user's laptop ever directly touches a plant-floor device. Addresses JIT access, MFA under plant-floor constraints, operator sovereignty, and session recording.
pillars.keyPrinciple: The Protocol Break is non-negotiable. A remote session must terminate in the IDMZ jump server, and a new independent session is opened from that jump server down to the OT asset. The user's laptop never has a routable path to Level 0–3.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Direct VPNs into plant floor, shared TeamViewer accounts, or dedicated jump servers
Internal IT/OT engineers, or external third-party OEMs and vendors
Mobile phones banned on plant floor; 24/7 manned control room
Plant Managers, Board, Auditors (NACSA s20)

planSections.title: The Protocol Break Architecture
keyTopics: IDMZ jump server: remote session terminates in IDMZ (VDI, Citrix, or secure reverse proxy) before a new session opens to the OT asset
Blocking direct internet dial-out: OT assets cannot initiate outbound sessions (eliminates rogue AnyDesk/TeamViewer)
Vendor-specific segmentation: Vendor A can only reach their specific PLC/HMI, not the broader OT network

planSections.title: Identity, MFA, and Privileged Access Management (PAM)
keyTopics: Hardware tokens (YubiKeys) where mobile phones are banned on the plant floor
Just-In-Time (JIT) access: access granted for a specific time window (e.g., 4-hour vendor maintenance session) and automatically revoked
Standing privileges eliminated: no permanent always-on vendor VPNs

planSections.title: Plant Floor Sovereignty — The Operator Override
keyTopics: Physical permissive: remote session cannot initiate unless a local operator approves via HMI workflow or physical key-switch
Session termination: operators can instantly kill any remote session if they observe unsafe physical behaviour in the process
Over-the-shoulder monitoring: local operators can see what the remote vendor is doing on their screen in real time

planSections.title: Session Monitoring & Auditing
keyTopics: Video-based session recording for all vendor sessions — stored in IDMZ, reviewed on-demand
Session metadata logging: who connected, from where, to which asset, for how long, what commands were executed
Anomaly alerting: sessions outside approved maintenance windows trigger immediate SOC alert

leading: % of third-party remote sessions using JIT access vs. standing always-on privileges (target: 100% JIT)
% of remote access sessions with session recording enabled
lagging: Number of rogue remote control tools (AnyDesk, TeamViewer outside approved gateway) detected and blocked
Average duration of vendor remote sessions vs. approved maintenance window duration
Number of sessions initiated without operator approval workflow

pillars.name: Training & Awareness
pillars.description: Design an impactful, role-based OT Training and Awareness programme that moves beyond generic IT phishing videos. Addresses cyber-physical anomaly recognition for operators, USB hygiene for maintenance technicians, and IT/OT severance authority for plant managers — delivered through plant-floor-appropriate mechanisms.
pillars.keyPrinciple: The most effective OT security awareness programme maps cyber threats to physical safety processes that operators already respect — not IT concepts. An operator who understands LOTO (Lock Out, Tag Out) already understands the concept of access control.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Generic IT phishing videos only, or OT-specific training already in place
Plant managers, control room operators, maintenance engineers, third-party vendors
12-hour shifts where 2-hour classroom training is impossible
HR, Plant Managers, NACSA Auditors

planSections.title: Role-Based Curriculum Design
keyTopics: Control Room Operators: recognising cyber-physical anomalies (HMI readings vs. physical gauge discrepancies), incident reporting procedures, dangers of bypassing safety interlocks
Maintenance / Instrument Techs: strict USB/removable media hygiene, secure handling of transient cyber assets (laptops, calibrators), Management of Change (MOC) awareness
Plant Managers / Leadership: cyber risk as operational downtime and physical safety risk, authority and procedure for authorising IT/OT network severance during an incident

planSections.title: Plant-Floor Delivery Mechanisms
keyTopics: Shift handover / toolbox talks: 3-minute micro-learning integrated into existing daily safety briefings
Visual campaigns: physical signage near high-risk areas ('STOP: Has this USB been scanned?' at engineering workstations)
IT/OT culture bridge: IT analysts shadow plant operations; automation engineers attend SOC sessions — mutual empathy building

planSections.title: OT Equivalents of the Phishing Test
keyTopics: Physical penetration / tailgating tests: do employees challenge unbadged personnel attempting to enter the control room?
USB drop exercises: benign USB drives left in plant breakroom — do they get plugged into production HMIs?
Vendor access checks: do operators verify a contractor's work order before unlocking a panel or enabling remote access?

planSections.title: Third-Party & Vendor Onboarding
keyTopics: Mandatory cyber site-briefing before any contractor is granted physical plant access
Prohibited equipment list: no personal devices, only approved transient assets
Acknowledgement sign-off capturing that the vendor understands OT cyber policies before access is granted

leading: % of contractors and vendors who completed the mandatory cyber site-briefing before plant access
% of shift handovers that included a security micro-briefing this month
lagging: Number of USB drop exercise drives that were plugged into production assets (target: 0)
Number of physical tailgating/social engineering attempts successfully challenged by operators
% of employees who can correctly identify the IT/OT severance trigger and escalation path

pillars.name: Risk Assessments
pillars.description: Design a foundational cyber-physical Risk Assessment programme using Consequence-Driven methodologies (IEC 62443-3-2, INL CCE). Starts with the physical consequence ('the Boom') rather than the IT vulnerability, and integrates with existing engineering safety processes like HAZOP and PHA.
pillars.keyPrinciple: Start with consequence, work backwards to cause. Identifying that a hacked chlorination dosing PLC could cause a public health emergency is more motivating — and more accurate — than citing a CVSS-9.8 score on an asset no one has heard of.

discoveryQuestions.example: Determines which consequence scenarios are most likely
NIST 800-30 for IT, HAZOP or PHA for engineering safety
Do you know which PLCs will cause total plant shutdown if compromised?
Untargeted ransomware, insider threats, or advanced state-sponsored activity
Board of Directors, Plant Engineering Leads, Insurers, NACSA

planSections.title: Consequence-Driven Identification — 'The Boom First'
keyTopics: Crown Jewel Analysis: work with plant engineers to identify worst-case physical outcomes (over-pressurisation, toxic release, loss of cooling, public water contamination)
Trace each consequence back to the specific digital assets controlling the relevant physical process
Engineering integration: map cyber risks directly into existing HAZOP or PHA documents

planSections.title: OT-Specific Threat Modelling & Likelihood
keyTopics: Threat profiling for converged actors: hacktivist speed/disruption tactics combined with state-sponsored OT capabilities
Reachability assessment: likelihood based on network segmentation — is the vulnerable PLC behind an IDMZ, or connected to an engineering workstation with dual NICs?
Sector-specific threat intelligence: known campaigns targeting your sector (e.g., water sector targeting by VOLTZITE)

planSections.title: Risk Treatment & Cyber-Informed Engineering (CIE)
keyTopics: Engineering out the risk: physical controls that cyber attacks cannot override (e.g., mechanical pressure relief valve so a hacked PLC physically cannot over-pressurise a tank)
Compensating controls with formal documentation and review triggers
Risk ownership: plant managers (business owners) must sign off on residual physical risk — not IT security

planSections.title: Dynamic Risk Register
keyTopics: Living register updated on MOC events — not a static annual spreadsheet
Risk ownership assigned to plant managers, not IT
Formal residual risk acceptance process with Board-level sign-off for Critical-band risks

leading: % of critical physical processes with a completed cyber-HAZOP assessment
% of risks in the register with an assigned plant-level owner (not IT owner)
lagging: Ratio of cyber risks mitigated via physical engineering controls vs. software patches
Number of high-risk findings in the register that lack an assigned owner or treatment plan

pillars.name: Real-Time Network Monitoring
pillars.description: Design a safety-first Network Security Monitoring (NSM) programme for OT. Passive-only data acquisition, industrial protocol deep packet inspection, OT-specific baselining, alert tuning, and a translation layer that bridges IT SOC analysts and plant-floor supervisors.
pillars.keyPrinciple: OT networks are deterministic — the same devices communicate with the same peers on the same ports at the same frequency. Anything outside the known-good baseline is immediately suspicious. This determinism makes OT the ideal environment for passive anomaly detection, but the fragility of legacy devices makes passive monitoring non-negotiable.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Completely blind at Purdue Levels 0–3, basic firewall logs, or full DPI via SPAN/TAPs
Internal IT SOC monitoring OT, outsourced MSSP, or dedicated OT SOC
Dragos/Claroty/Nozomi, or open-source Zeek/Suricata/Wireshark for PCAP analysis
CISO, Plant Managers, external NACSA regulators

planSections.title: Safety-First Passive Architecture
keyTopics: TAPs vs. SPANs: physical Network TAPs at IT/OT boundary and IDMZ; SPAN/mirror ports at core OT switches
Deep Packet Inspection for industrial protocols: Modbus TCP, DNP3, ENIP/CIP, S7Comm, BACnet — detect malicious setpoint changes or unauthorised PLC logic downloads
100% passive, listen-only posture — sensors must never inject traffic that could crash fragile legacy PLCs

planSections.title: Baselining & Threat Modelling — IT/OT Convergence
keyTopics: Asset communication baseline: matrix of who talks to whom, on what port, at what frequency — deviations are immediate anomalies
Hunting the converged actor: detection hypotheses for state-sponsored TTPs using hacktivist-speed disruption tools
Historian polling baselines: filter expected OSIsoft PI polling traffic so it does not generate false positives

planSections.title: Alert Tuning & the IT-to-Plant Escalation Workflow
keyTopics: Tuning false positives: filter known-good traffic patterns (scheduled backups, historian polling, PLC health checks)
Translation layer: playbook mapping SOC alert types to plain-English questions for plant supervisors (e.g., 'Modbus FC16 Write detected on Reactor PLC at 02:14 — was maintenance scheduled?')
Escalation path: Level 1 SOC → OT Security Engineer → Plant Shift Supervisor → Plant Manager

planSections.title: Incident Data Retention
keyTopics: Full PCAP storage strategy vs. NetFlow for cost-managed long-term retention
Retention periods: 90 days full PCAP at network choke points; 12 months NetFlow for trend analysis
Forensic preservation: when to freeze a PCAP archive on suspected incident detection

leading: % of Purdue Level 2/3 network choke points with passive monitoring sensor coverage
% of known OT communication baselines formally documented
lagging: False Positive to True Positive alert ratio (target: below 5:1 after tuning period)
Time-to-Acknowledge by plant-floor personnel for critical OT alerts (target: < 30 minutes during business hours)

pillars.name: Teamwork — IT/OT Partnership
pillars.description: Design a strategic blueprint for building a high-performing, cross-functional IT/OT Cybersecurity Team. Addresses governance structure, cross-pollination programmes, hybrid role definitions, RACI matrices, and fostering a no-blame incident culture that bridges the IT/OT cultural divide.
pillars.keyPrinciple: You cannot secure what you do not understand. An IT analyst who has never walked a plant floor will make architectural decisions that are technically correct but operationally catastrophic. Mandatory cross-training is not a soft HR initiative — it is a security control.

discoveryQuestions.example: Manufacturing, Power Generation, Water/Wastewater, Oil & Gas
Highly siloed and combative, passively cooperative, or moving toward unified model
Does the enterprise CISO own OT cyber risk, or does the Plant/Site Manager?
Dedicated OT security engineers, or IT network + automation engineers doubling up
CISO, VP of Operations, HR

planSections.title: The IT/OT Centre of Excellence (Steering Committee)
keyTopics: Joint charter: mission statement explicitly balancing IT security mandates with OT safety and reliability mandates
Governance structure: CISO + Plant Director + Engineering Lead + OT Security Architect as voting members
Tie-breaker workflow: when IT demands a patch and OT refuses due to production schedule — who holds the veto, and how is residual risk formally accepted?

planSections.title: Cross-Pollination and Empathy Building
keyTopics: IT in the Plant: IT security analysts walk the plant floor, shadow shift supervisors, witness physical processes and LOTO procedures
OT in the SOC: control engineers sit in the IT Security Operations Centre to understand network telemetry and threat intelligence
Mandatory annual cross-training hours for both disciplines

planSections.title: Defining Hybrid Roles & Responsibilities
keyTopics: The OT Security Architect: translates enterprise security policy into Purdue-compliant architectures; must understand both firewall rules and ladder logic
The Automation/Process Security Analyst: plant-side engineer who acts as the primary liaison during incidents — holds the 'physical wrench' while IT holds the 'keyboard'
RACI matrix for key activities: firewall rule changes, vulnerability scanning, incident containment, MOC approvals

planSections.title: Fostering a No-Blame Incident Culture
keyTopics: Joint post-incident and post-TTX debriefs structured to prevent IT/OT finger-pointing
Psychological safety: plant operators must feel comfortable reporting suspected anomalies (unusual HMI mouse movements, unexpected valve activations) without fear of reprimand from IT
Near-miss reporting system adapted from existing plant safety culture

leading: % of IT security staff with completed plant floor safety/HAZOP familiarisation hours
Number of jointly authored MOC tickets (signed off by both IT Security and Plant Engineering)
lagging: Reduction in escalations to the Steering Committee for IT/OT dispute resolution (indicates improved working relationship)
% of post-incident Action Items that have joint IT and OT ownership
